There is a net flow of heat between two points if there is a temperature difference between them. The electrical equivalent of this is that there is only a net current i between two points if there is a potential difference v between them, the relationship between the current and potential difference being i = v/R, where R is the electrical resistance between the points. A similar relationship can be used to define thermal resistance R. If q is the rate of flow of heat and (T - T ) temperature difference, then 1 2 T - T 2 1 q = R The value of the resistance depends on the mode of heat transfer. 472 CHAPTER 17 BASIC SYSTEM MODELS Summary A mathematical model of a system is a description of it in terms of equations relating inputs and outputs so that outputs can be predicted from inputs. Mechanical systems can be considered to be made up from masses, springs and dashpots, or moments of inertia, springs and rotational dampers if rotational. Electrical systems can be considered to be made up from resistors, capacitors and inductors, hydraulic and pneumatic systems from resistance, capacitance and inertance, and thermal systems from resistance and capacitance. There are many elements in mechanical, electrical, fluid and thermal systems which have similar behaviours. Thus, for example, mass in mechanical systems has similar properties to capacitance in electrical systems, capacitance in fluid systems and capacitance in thermal systems. Table 17.5 shows a comparison of the elements in each of these systems and their defining equations. Table 17.5 System elements. System models Chapter eighteen Objectives The objectives of this chapter are that, after studying it, the reader should be able to: • Devise models for rotational–translational, electromechanical and hydraulic–mechanical systems. • Linearise non-linear relationships in order to generate linear models. 18.1 Engineering systems In Chapter 17 the basic building blocks of translational mechanical, rotational mechanical, electrical, fluid and thermal systems were separately considered. However, many systems encountered in engineering involve aspects of more than one of these disciplines. For example, an electric motor involves both electrical and mechanical elements. This chapter looks at how singlediscipline building blocks can be combined to give models for such multidiscipline systems and also addresses the issue that often real components are not linear. For example, in considering a spring the simple model assumes that the force and extension are proportional, regardless of how large the force was. The mathematical model might thus be a simplification of a real spring. Non-linear models are, however, much more difficult to deal with and so engineers try to avoid them and a non-linear system might be approximated by a linear model. 18.2 Rotational– translational There are many mechanisms which involve the conversion of rotational systems motion to translational motion or vice versa. For example, there are rack-andpinion, shafts with lead screws, pulley and cable systems, etc. To illustrate how such systems can be analysed, consider a rack-and-pinion system (Figure 18.1). The rotational motion of the pinion is transformed into translational motion of the rack. Consider first the pinion element. The net torque acting on it is (T - T ). Thus, considering the moment of inertia in out element, and assuming negligible damping, dv T - T = I in out dt M18 Mechatronics 50977.indd 475 29/08/2018 18:07

476 CHAPTER 18 SYSTEM MODELS Input Output, where I is the moment of inertia of the pinion and v its angular velocity. Summary Many systems encountered in engineering involve aspects of more than one discipline and these can be considered by examining how the system can be built up from single-discipline building blocks. A system is said to be linear when its basic equations, whether algebraic or differential, are such that the magnitude of the output produced is directly proportional to the input. For an algebraic equation, this means that the graph of output plotted against input is a straight line passing through the origin. So doubling the input doubles the output. For a linear system we can obtain the output of the system to a number of inputs by adding the outputs of the system to each individual input considered separately. This is called the principle of superposition. Dynamic responses of Chapter nineteen systems Objectives The objectives of this chapter are that, after studying it, the reader should be able to: • Model dynamic systems by means of differential equations. • Determine the outputs of first-order systems to inputs and determine time constants. • Determine the outputs of second-order systems to inputs and identify the under-damped, critically damped and over-damped conditions. • Describe the characteristics of second-order system responses in terms of rise time, overshoot, subsidence ratio, decrement and settling time. 19.1 Modelling dynamic The most important function of a model devised for measurement or control systems systems is to be able to predict what the output will be for a particular input. We are not just concerned with a static situation, i.e. that after some time when the steady state has been reached an output of x corresponds to an input of y. We have to consider how the output will change with time when there is a change of input or when the input changes with time. For example, how will the temperature of a temperature-controlled system change with time when the thermostat is set to a new temperature? For a control system, how will the output of the system change with time when the set value is set to a new value or perhaps increased at a steady rate? Chapters 17 and 18 were concerned with models of systems when the inputs varied with time, with the results being expressed in terms of differential equations. This chapter is about how we can use such models to make predictions about how outputs will change with time when the input changes with time. 19.1.1 Differential equations To describe the relationship between the input to a system and its output we must describe the relationship between inputs and outputs which are both possible functions of time. We thus need a form of equation which will indicate how the system output will vary with time when the input is varying with time. This can be done by the use of a differential equation. Such an equation M19 Mechatronics 50977.indd 485 29/08/2018 18:09

486 CHAPTER 19 DYNAMIC RESPONSES OF SYSTEMS includes derivatives with respect to time and so gives information about how the response of a system varies with time. A derivative dx dt describes the rate at which x varies with time; the derivative d2x dt2 states how dx dt varies with time. Differential equations can be classed as first-order, >second-order, thirdorder, etc., according to the highest order of> the derivative in> the equation. For a first-order equation the highest order will be dx dt, with a second-order d2x dt2, with a third-order d3x dt3, with nth-order dnx dtn. This chapter is about the types of responses we can> expect from first-order and> second-order systems and >the solution of such diff>erential equations so that the response of the system to different types of input can be obtained. This chapter uses the ‘try a solution’ approach to find a solution; the Laplace transformation method is introduced in Chapter 20. 19.2 Terminology In this section we look at some of the terms that are used in describing the dynamic responses of systems. 19.2.1 Natural and forced responses The term natural response is used for a system when there is no input to the system forcing the variable to change but it is just changing naturally. As an illustration, consider the first-order system of water being allowed naturally to flow out of a tank (Figure 19.1(a)). L 19.2.2 Transient and steady-state responses The total response of a control system, or element of a system, can be considered to be made up of two aspects, the steady-state response and the transient response. The transient response is that part of a system response which occurs as a result of a change in input and which dies away after a short interval of time. The steady-state response is the response that remains after all transient responses have died down. To give a simple illustration of this, consider a vertically suspended spring (Figure 19.2) and what happens when a weight is suddenly suspended from it. The deflection of the spring abruptly increases and it then may well oscillate until after some time it settles down to a steady value. The steady value is the steady-state response of the spring system; the oscillation that occurs prior to this steady state is the transient response. This type of input is known as a step input and is of the form shown in Figure 19.3(a). The input signal to systems can take other forms, e.g. impulse, ramp and sinusoidal signals. An impulse is a very short-duration input (Figure 19.3(b)); a ramp is a steadily increasing input (Figure 19.3(c)) and can be described by an equation of the form y = kt, where k is a constant; and a sinusoidal input can be described by an equation of the form y = k sin vt, with v being the so-called angular frequency and equal to 2pf, where f is the frequency. Both the input and the output are functions of time. One way of indicating this is to write them in the form f (t), where f is the function and (t) indicates that its value depends on time t. Thus for the weight W input to the spring system we could write W(t) and for the deflection d output d(t). Thus if the force was applied as a step input and there was no damping at all then the mass would freely oscillate on the spring and the oscillations would continue indefinitely. No damping means c = 0 and so the dx dt term is zero. However, damping will cause the oscillations to die away until a steady displacement of the mass is obtained. If the damping is high eno>ugh there will be no oscillations and the displacement of the mass will just slowly increase with time and gradually the mass will move towards its steady displacement position. Figure 19.9 shows the general way that the displacements, for a step input, vary with time with different degrees of damping. 19.4.1 Natural response Consider a mass on the end of a spring. This process of determining a mathematical model is known as system identification. Thus if we obtain a response to a step input of the form shown in Figure 19.5 then we might assume that it is a first-order system and determine the time constant from the response curve. SUMMARY 505 dx 1.5 + x = 5y dt An under-damped second-order system will give a response to a step input of the form shown in Figure 19.12. The damping ratio can be determined from measurements of the first and second overshoots with the ratio of these overshoots, i.e. the subsidence ratio, giving the damping ratio. Summary The natural response of a system is when there is no input to the system forcing the variable to change but it is just changing naturally. The forced response of a system is when there is an input to the system forcing it to change. System transfer Chapter twenty functions Objectives The objectives of this chapter are that, after studying it, the reader should be able to: • Define the transfer function and determine it from differential equations for first- and second-order systems. • Determine the transfer functions for systems with feedback loops. • Determine, using Laplace transforms, the responses of first- and second-order systems to simple inputs. • Determine the effect of pole location on the responses of systems. 20.1 The transfer function For an amplifier system it is customary to talk of the gain of the a mplifier. This states how much bigger the output signal will be when compared with the input signal. It enables the output to be determined for specific inputs. Thus, for example, an amplifier with a voltage gain of 10 will give, for an input voltage of 2 mV, an output of 20 mV; or if the input is 1 V an output of 10 V. The gain states the mathematical relationship between the output and the input for the block. We can indicate when a signal is in the time domain, i.e. is a function of time, by writing it as f(t). Thus, for an input of y(t) and an output of x(t) (Figure 20.1(a)), output x(t) gain = = input y(t) However, for many systems the relationship between the output and the input is in the form of a differential equation and so a statement of the function as just a simple number like the gain of 10 is not possible. We cannot just divide the output by the input because the relationship is a differential equation and not a simple algebraic equation. We can, however, transform a differential equation into an algebraic equation by using what is termed the Laplace transform. Differential equations describe how systems behave with time and are transformed by means of the Laplace transform into simple algebraic equations, not involving time, where we can carry out normal algebraic manipulations of the quantities. We talk of behaviour in the time domain being transformed to the s -domain. When in the s-domain a function is written, since it is a function of s, as F(s). It is usual to use a capital letter F for the Laplace transform and a lower case letter f for the time-varying function f(t). We then define the relationship between output and input in terms of a transfer function, this stating the relationship between the Laplace transform of the output and the Laplace transform of the input. 20.6 EFFECT OF POLE LOCATION ON TRANSIENT RESPONSE 519 Neglecting small quantities this becomes 0.5 T(s) = 0.002s2 + 0.050s + 0.625 20.6 Effect of pole location on We can define a system as being stable if, when it is given an input, it has transient transients which die away with time and leave the system in its steady-state response condition. A system is said to be unstable if the transients do not die away with time but increase in size and so the steady-state condition is never attained. Consider an input of a unit impulse to a first-order system with a transfer function of G(s) = 1 (s + 1). The system output X(s) is 1> X(s) = * 1 s + 1 and thus x = e-t. As the time t increases, so the output dies away eventually to become zero. Now consider the unit impulse input to a system with the transfer function G(s) = 1 (s - 1). The output is 1 > X(s) = * 1 s - 1 and so x = et. As t increases, so the output increases with time. Thus a momentary impulse to the system results in an ever increasing output; this system is unstable. For a transfer function, the values of s which make the transfer function infinite are termed its poles; they are the roots of the characteristic equation. Thus for G(s) = 1 (s + 1), there is a pole of s = -1. For G(s) = 1 (s - 1) there is a pole of s = +1. Thus, for a first-order system, the system is stable if the pole is negati>ve and unstable if the pole is positive (Figure 20>.7). For a second-order system with transfer function b v2 0 n G(s) = s2 + 2zv s + v2 n n when subject to a unit impulse input, b v2 0 n X(s) = (s + p )(s + p ) 1 2 where p and p are the roots of the equation 1 2 s2 + 2zv s + v = 0 n n Figure 20.7 First-order systems: x x (a) negative pole, (b) positive pole. 0 0 t t (a) (b) M20 Mechatronics 50977.indd 519 29/08/2018 18:11

520 CHAPTER 20 SYSTEM TRANSFER FUNCTIONS Figure 20.8 Second-order x x systems. 0 0 t t 0 0 (a) (b) Using the equation for the roots of a quadratic equation, p = -2zv n { 22 4z2v n 2 - 4v n 2 = -zv n{v n z2 - 1 2 Depending on the value of the damping factor, the term under the square root sign can be real or imaginary. When there is an imaginary term the output involves an oscillation. For example, suppose we have a second-order system with transfer function 1 G(s) = [s - (-2 + j1)][s - (-2 - j1)] i.e. p = +2 { j1. When subject to a unit impulse input the output is e-2t sin t. The amplitude of the oscillation, i.e. e-2t, dies away as the time increases and so the effect of the impulse is a gradually decaying oscillation (Figure 20.8(a)). The system is stable. Suppose, however, we have a system with transfer function 1 G(s) = [s - (2 + j1)][s - (2 - j1)] i.e. p = +2 { j1. When subject to a unit impulse input, the output is e2t sin t. The amplitude of the oscillation, i.e. e2t, increases as the time increases (Figure 20.8(b)). The system is unstable. In general, when an impulse is applied to a system, the output is in the form of the sum of a number of exponential terms. If just one of these terms is of exponential growth then the output continues to grow and the system is unstable. When there are pairs of poles involving plus or minus imaginary terms then the output is an oscillation. A system is stable if the real part of all its poles is negative. A system is unstable if the real part of any of its poles is positive. 20.6.1 The s-plane We can plot the positions of the poles of a system on a graph with the x-axis being the real parts and the y-axis the imaginary parts. Such a graph is termed the s-plane. The location of the poles on the plane determines the stability of a system. Figure 20.9 shows such a plane and how the location of roots affects the response of a system. M20 Mechatronics 50977.indd 520 29/08/2018 18:11

Systems can have their responses to inputs altered by including compensators. A compensator is a block which is incorporated in the system so that it alters the overall transfer function of the system in such a way as to obtain the required characteristics. As an illustration of the use of a compensator, consider a position control system which has a negative feedback path with a transfer function of 1 and two subsystems in its forward path: a compensator with a transfer function of K and a motor drive system with a transfer function of 1 s(s + 1). Frequency response Chapter twenty-one Objectives The objectives of this chapter are that, after studying it, the reader should be able to: • Explain the meaning of the frequency-response function. • Analyse the frequency response of systems subject to sinusoidal inputs. • Plot and interpret Bode plots. • Use Bode plots for system identification. • Explain the term bandwidth. • Explain how the gain margin and phase margin can be used to indicate the stability of a system. 21.1 Sinusoidal input In the previous two chapters, the response of systems to step, impulse and ramp inputs has been considered. This chapter extends this to when there is a sinusoidal input. While for many control systems a sinusoidal input might not be encountered normally, it is a useful testing input since the way the system responds to such an input is a very useful source of information to aid the design and analysis of systems. It is also useful because many other signals can be considered as the sum of a number of sinusoidal signals. In 1822 Jean Baptiste Fourier proposed that any periodic waveform, e.g. a square waveform, can be made up of a combination of sinusoidal waveforms and by considering the behaviour of a system to each individual sinusoidal waveform it is possible to determine the response to the more complex waveform. 21.1.1 Response of a system to a sinusoidal input Consider a first-order system which is described by the differential equation dx a + a x = b y 1dt 0 0 where y is the input and x the output. Suppose we have the unit amplitude sinusoidal input of y = sin vt. What will the output be? Well we must end up with the sinusoid b sin vt when we add a dx dt and a x. But sinusoids have the 0 1 0 property that when differentiated, the result is also a sinusoid and with the same frequency (a cosine is a sinusoid, being just >sin(vt + 90°). This applies no matter how many times we carry out the differentiation. Thus we should expect that the steady-state response x will also be sinusoidal and with the same frequency. The output may, however, differ in amplitude and phase from the input. M21 Mechatronics 50977.indd 524 29/08/2018 18:14

21.2 PHASORS 525 21.2 Phasors In discussing sinusoidal signals it is convenient to use phasors. Consider a sinusoid described by the equation v = V sin(vt + f), where V is the amplitude, v the angular frequency and f the phase angle. The phasor can be represented by a line of length (cid:30)V(cid:30) making an initial angle of f with the phase reference axis (Figure 21.1). The (cid:30) (cid:30) lines are used to indicate that we are only concerned with the magnitude or size of the quantity when specifying its length. To specify a phasor quantity completely requires a magnitude and an angle to be stated. The convention generally adopted is to write a phasor in bold, non-italic, print, e.g. V. When such a symbol is seen it implies a quantity having both a magnitude and an angle. Such a phasor can be described by means of complex number notation. A complex quantity can be represented by (x + jy), where x is the real part and y the imaginary part of the complex number. On a graph with the imaginary component as the y-axis and the real part as the x-axis, x and y are Cartesian co-ordinates of the point representing the complex number (Figure 21.2(a)). If we take the line joining this point to the graph origin to represent a phasor, then we have the phase angle f of the phasor represented by y tan f = x and its length by the use of Pythagoras’ theorem as length of phasor (cid:30)V(cid:30) = x2 + y2 2 Figure 21.1 Representing a Angle rotated Angular velocity sinusoidal signal by a phasor. in time t is v vt V |V| f A f O Starting with 90° 180° 270° 360° 450° initial phase Angle relative angle f to axis OA, hence time y 0 x Real Figure 21.2 (a) Complex representation of a phasor, (b) 0°, (c) 90°, (d) 270°, (e) 360°. yranigamI y + jx Imaginary Imaginary Imaginary Imaginary |V| 0 Real 0 Real 0 Real 0 Real f (a) (b) (c) (d) (e) M21 Mechatronics 50977.indd 525 29/08/2018 18:14

526 CHAPTER 21 FREQUENCY RESPONSE Since x = (cid:30)V(cid:30) cos f and y = (cid:30)V(cid:30) sin f, then we can write V = x + jy = (cid:30)V(cid:30)cos u + j(cid:30)V(cid:30)sin u = (cid:30)V(cid:30)(cos u + j sin u) Thus a specification of the real and imaginary parts of a complex number enables a phasor to be specified. Consider a phasor of length 1 and phase angle 0° (Figure 21.2(b)). It will have a complex representation of 1 + j0. Now consider the same length phasor but with a phase angle of 90° (Figure 21.2(c)). It will have a complex representation of 0 + j1. Thus rotation of a phasor anti-clockwise by 90° corresponds to multiplication of the phasor by j. If we now rotate this phasor by a further 90° (Figure 21.2(d)), then following the same multiplication rule we have the original phasor multiplied by j2. But the phasor is just the original phasor in the opposite direction, i.e. just multiplied by -1. Hence j2 = -1 and so j = (-1). Rotation of the original phasor through a total of 270°, i.e. 3 * 90°, 2is equivalent to multiplying the original phasor by j3 = j(j2) = -j. To illustrate the above, consider a voltage which varies sinusoidally with time according to the equation v = 10 sin(vt + 30∘) V When represented by a phasor, what are (a) its length, (b) its angle relative to the reference axis, (c) its real and imaginary parts when represented by a complex number? (a) The phasor will have a length scaled to represent the amplitude of the sinusoid and so is 10 V. (b) The angle of the phasor relative to the reference axis is equal to the phase angle and so is 30°. (c) The real part is given by the equation x = 10 cos 30° = 8.7 V and the imaginary part by y = 10 sin 30° = 5.0 V. Thus the phasor is specified by 8.7 + j5.0 V. 21.2.1 Phasor equations Consider a phasor as representing the unit amplitude sinusoid of x = sin vt. Differentiation of the sinusoid gives dx dt = v cos vt. But we can also write this as dx dt = v sin(vt + 90°). In other words, differentiation just results in a phasor with a length increased by a >factor of v and which is rotated round by 90° fro>m the original phasor. Thus, in complex notation, we have multiplied the original phasor by jv, since multiplication by j is equivalent to a rotation through 90°. Thus the differential equation dx a + a x = b y 1dt 0 0 can be written, in complex notation, as a phasor equation jva X + a X = b Y 1 0 0 where the bold, non-italic, letters indicate that the data refers to phasors. We can say that the differential equation, which was an equation in the time M21 Mechatronics 50977.indd 526 29/08/2018 18:14

538 CHAPTER 21 FREQUENCY RESPONSE 21.4.6 System identification If we experimentally determine the Bode diagram for a system by considering its response to a sinusoidal input, then we can obtain the transfer function for the system. Basically we draw the asymptotes on the magnitude Bode plot and consider their gradients. The phase angle curve is used to check the results obtained from the magnitude analysis. 1 If the gradient at low frequencies prior to the first corner frequency is zero then there is no s or 1 s element in the transfer function. The K element in the numerator of the transfer function can be obtained from the value of the low-frequency m>agnitude; the magnitude in dB = 20 lg K. 2 If the initial gradient at low frequencies is -20 dB decade then the transfer function has a 1 s element. 3 If the gradient becomes more negative at a corne>r frequency by 20 dB decade, there is a (1>+ s v ) term in the denominator of the transfer funcc tion, with v being the corner frequency at which the change occurs. Such> c terms can occur for mor>e than one corner frequency. 4 If the gradient becomes more positive at a corner frequency by 20 dB decade, there is a (1 + s v ) term in the numerator of the transfer funcc tion, with v being the frequency at which the change occurs. Such term>s c can occur for more than o>ne corner frequency. 5 If the gradient at a corner frequency becomes more negative by 40 dB decade, there is a (s2 v2 + 2zs v + 1) term in the denominator of the c c transfer function. The damping ratio z can be found by considering the> detail of the Bode plo>t at a corne>r frequency, as in Figure 21.6. 6 If the gradient at a corner frequency becomes more positive by 40 dB decade, there is a (s2 v2 + 2zs v + 1) term in the numerator of the c c transfer function. 21.6 STABILITY 541 Figure 21.13 Self-sustaining oscillations. 0 Time rorre laitinI 0 Time rorre weN 0 Time langis kcabdeF 21.6 Stability When there is a sinusoidal input to a system, the output from that system is sinusoidal with the same angular frequency but can have an output with an amplitude and phase which differ from that of the input. Consider a closedloop system with negative feedback (Figure 21.13) and no input to the system. Suppose, somehow, we have a half-rectified sinusoidal pulse as the error signal in the system and that it passes through to the output and is fed back to arrive at the comparator element with amplitude unchanged but delayed by just half a cycle, i.e. a phase change of 180° as shown in the figure. When this signal is subtracted from the input signal, we have a resulting error signal which just continues the initial half-rectified pulse. This pulse then goes back round the feedback loop and once again arrives just in time to continue the signal. Thus we have a self-sustaining oscillation. For self-sustained oscillations to occur we must have a system which has a frequency-response function with a magnitude of 1 and a phase of -180°. The system through which the signal passes is G(s) in series with H(s). If the magnitude is less than 1 then each succeeding half-wave pulse is smaller in size and so the oscillation dies away. If the magnitude is greater than 1 then each succeeding pulse is larger than the previous one and so the wave builds up and the system is unstable. 1 A control system will oscillate with a constant amplitude if the magnitude resulting from the system G(s) in series with H(s) is 1 and the phase is -180°. 2 A control system will oscillate with a diminishing amplitude if the magnitude resulting from the system G(s) in series with H(s) is less than 1 and the phase is -180°. 3 A control system will oscillate with an increasing amplitude, and so is unstable, if the magnitude resulting from the system G(s) in series with H(s) is greater than 1 and the phase is -180°. A good, stable control system usually requires that the magnitude of G(s)H(s) should be significantly less than 1. Typically a value between 0.4 and 0.5 is used. In addition, the phase angle should be between about -115° and -125°. Such values produce a slightly under-damped control system which gives, Error Output G(s) H(s) Feedback M21 Mechatronics 50977.indd 541 29/08/2018 18:14

542 CHAPTER 21 FREQUENCY RESPONSE Figure 21.14 Bode plot showing gain crossover and phase crossover. with a step input, about a 20 to 30% overshoot with a subsidence ratio of about 3 to 1 (see Section 19.5 for an explanation of these terms). A system may be designed with a G(s)H(s) product which gives a stable system, but a vital question is how much change in amplitude and phase the product can withstand before the overall system becomes unstable. The Bode plot of an open-loop system provides a method by which the stability of a system can be determined (Figure 21.14) and how close it is to becoming unstable, the measures used being: 1 Gain crossover This is the frequency at which the open-loop gain first reaches 1, i.e. 0 dB. The gain margin is the factor by which the gain must be multiplied at the gain crossover point to have the value 1. 2 Phase crossover This is the frequency at which the phase angle first reaches -180°. The phase margin is the number of degrees by which the phase angle is smaller than -180° at the gain crossover. For safety the gain margin must be a safe amount less than 1, i.e. 0 dB. Standard practice is a gain margin of 6 dB. Also, the phase margin must be a safe amount above -180°; standard practice is 40°. A closed-loop control system is deemed stable with these conditions. It is deemed unstable if the open-loop gain is 1 or greater at the frequency at which the phase is -180°. Both the gain crossover and the phase crossover need to be right for the system to be stable, though one of the tests not being met is enough to prove instability. In designing control circuits, if the performance is not right an additional element, termed a compensator, can be included. For a simple, single-loop feedback system, the compensator can be inserted in the forward loop of the control system (Figure 21.15(a)). These are used to improve stability by modifying the frequency response of the system. For example, if a system is unstable or too close to unstable because of the phase margin not being adequately above -180°, moving the open-loop gain graph line to the left would give a better phase margin. This can be achieved by introducing a lag-compensation element in the forward path of the control system. Figure 21.15(b) shows the effect of phase-lag compensation. Lag compensation is designed to move the )Bd( niag pool-nepO 0 0 esahP Gain crossover v Gain margin v Phase crossover −180° Phase margin M21 Mechatronics 50977.indd 542 29/08/2018 18:14

However, it also usually increases the time constant of the system and as a result the system becomes more sluggish. A lead-compensation element with the controller can be used to improve the stability by directly increasing the phase at the crossover point, also improving the speed of response. 544 CHAPTER 21 FREQUENCY RESPONSE The frequency response of a system is the set of values of the magnitude (cid:30)G(jv)(cid:30) and phase angle f that occur when a sinusoidal input signal is varied over a range of frequencies. This can be expressed as two graphs, one of the magnitude (cid:30)G(jv)(cid:30) plotted against the angular frequency v and the other of the phase f plotted against v. The magnitude and angular frequency are plotted using logarithmic scales. Such a pair of graphs is referred to as a Bode plot. We can obtain the Bode plot of a system by adding together the Bode plots of the magnitudes of the constituent elements. Likewise the phase plot is obtained by adding together the phases of the constituent elements. The peak resonance M is the maximum value of the magnitude. The p bandwidth is the frequency band between which the magnitude does not fall below -3 dB, the frequencies at which this occurs being termed the cut-off frequencies. For self-sustained oscillations to occur with a feedback system, i.e. it is on the verge of instability, we must have a system which has a frequencyresponse function with a magnitude of 1 and a phase of -180°. The gain margin is the factor by which the magnitude ratio must be multiplied when the phase is -180° to make it have the value 1 and so be on the verge of instability. The phase margin is the number of degrees by which the phase angle is numerically smaller than -180° when the magnitude is 1. Closed-loop controllers Chapter twenty two Objectives The objectives of this chapter are that, after studying it, the reader should be able to: • Explain the term steady-state error. • Explain the operation of the two-step mode of control. • Predict the behaviour of systems with proportional, integral, derivative, proportional plus integral, proportional plus derivative and PID control. • Describe how digital controllers operate. • Explain how controllers can be tuned. 22.1 Control processes Open-loop control (see Section 1.5.2) is often just a switch-on–switch-off form of control, e.g. an electric fire is either switched on or off to heat a room and obtain the required temperature. In open-loop systems the controller sends a command through possibly an amplifier to the actuator and does not know if the command results in the desired outcome. For example, for motion control a d.c. motor might be given a controlling input from a microcontroller via an H-bridge driver to achieve some motion control, but with this just being openloop control it is not known whether the required motion is achieved. There is no feedback to indicate whether it has been achieved. With closed-loop control systems (see Section 1.5.2), a controller is used to compare the output of a system with the required condition and convert the error from the required condition into a control action designed to reduce the error. The error might arise because of some change in the conditions being controlled or because the set value is changed, e.g. there is a step input to the system to change the set value to a new value. In this chapter we are concerned with the ways in which controllers can react to error signals, i.e. the control modes as they are termed, which occur with continuous processes. The term direct digital control is used when a computer or microcontroller is in the feedback loop and exercising the control (see Section 1.5.4). In this chapter such control is discussed. Many processes not only involve control of some variable, e.g. temperature, to a required value, but also involve the sequencing of operations. A domestic washing machine (see Section 1.5.5) where a number of actions have to be carried out in a predetermined sequence is an example. Another example is the manufacture of a product which involves the assembly of a number of discrete parts in a specific sequence by some controlled system. The sequence of operations might be clock-based or event-based or a combination of the two. With a clock-based system the actions are carried out at specific times; with an event-based system the actions are carried out when there is feedback to indicate that a particular event has occurred. M22 Mechatronics 50977.indd 546 29/08/2018 18:16

22.1 CONTROL PROCESSES 547 In many processes there can be a mixture of continuous and discrete control. For example, in the domestic washing machine there will be sequence control for the various parts of the washing cycle with feedback loop control of the temperature of the hot water and the level of the water. 22.1.1 Terminology The following are terms commonly used in discussing controllers: 1 Lag In any control system, there are lags. Thus, for example, a change in the condition being controlled does not immediately produce a correcting response from the control system. This is because time is required for the system to make the necessary responses. For example, in the control of the temperature in a room by means of a central heating system, a lag will occur between the room temperature falling below the required temperature and the control system responding and switching on the heater. This is not the only lag. Even when the control system has responded, there is a lag in the room temperature responding as time is taken for the heat to transfer from the heater to the air in the room. 2 Steady-state error A closed-loop control system uses a measurement of the system output and a comparison of its value with the desired output to generate an error signal. We might get an error signal to the controller occurring as a result of the controlled variable changing or a change in the set value input. For example, we might have a step input to the system with the aim that the controlled variable output rapidly reaches the set value. When the step input change occurs, there are likely to be some transient effects; these, however, die away with time. Ideally the system output tracks the input of the desired set value signal and there is no difference between them. However, this situation is rarely met. Figure 22.1(a) illustrates this for a step input. The term Figure 22.1 (a) Steady-state Output signal with no error with a step input, (b) error Step input steady-state error compensation. signal Signal Steady-state error size Output signal with steady-state error error Time 0 (a) Set value Error Output Compensator/ + Plant controller − Feedback (b) M22 Mechatronics 50977.indd 547 29/08/2018 18:16

548 CHAPTER 22 CLOSED-LOOP CONTROLLERS steady-state error is used for the difference between the desired set value input and the output after all transients have died away. It is thus a measure of the accuracy of the control system in tracking the set value input. Whenever there is a steady-state error, the output is not at the desired output. Compensation is often included in the forward path of a closed-loop control loop (Figure 22.1(b)) in the design of the controller to improve its performance and reduce the steady-state error and is termed cascade compensation. One method used is for the compensator/controller to have an integrating action (see Section 22.4 for a discussion of integration). 22.1.2 Control modes There are a number of ways by which a control unit can react to an error signal and supply an output for correcting elements: 1 The two-step mode, often termed the on/off mode, in which the controller is essentially just a switch which is activated by the error signal and supplies just an on/off correcting signal. 2 The proportional mode (P) which produces a control action that is proportional to the error. The correcting signal thus becomes bigger, the bigger the error. Thus, as the error is reduced the amount of correction is reduced and the correcting process slows down. 3 The derivative mode (D) which produces a control action that is proportional to the rate at which the error is changing. When there is a sudden change in the error signal the controller gives a large correcting signal; when there is a gradual change only a small correcting signal is produced. Derivative control can be considered to be a form of anticipatory control in that the existing rate of change of error is measured, a coming larger error is anticipated, and correction is applied before the larger error has arrived. Derivative control is not used alone but always in conjunction with proportional control and, often, integral control. 4 The integral mode (I) which produces a control action that is proportional to the integral of the error with time. Thus, a constant error signal will produce an increasing correcting signal. The correction continues to increase as long as the error persists. The integral controller can be considered to be ‘looking back’, summing all the errors and thus responding to changes that have occurred. 5 Combinations of modes: proportional plus derivative (PD) modes, proportional plus integral (PI) modes, proportional plus integral plus derivative (PID) modes. The term three-term controller is used for PID control. These five modes of control are discussed in the following sections of the chapter. 22.2 Two-step or on/ off mode An example of the two-step mode of control is the bimetallic thermostat (see Figure 2.46) that might be used with a simple temperature control system. This is just a switch which is switched on or off according to the temperature. If the room temperature is above the required temperature, then the bimetallic strip is in an off position and the

bimetallic thermostat (see Figure 2.46) that might be used with a simple temperature control system. This is just a switch which is switched on or off according to the temperature. If the room temperature is above the required temperature, then the bimetallic strip is in an off position and the heater is off. If the room temperature falls below the required temperature, then the bimetallic strip moves into an on position and the heater is switched fully on. 22.2 TWO-STEP OR ON/OFF MODE 549 Heater supply Dead band Switch Switch off on Off Temperature Controller switch points (c) Figure 22.2 Two-step control. in only two positions, on or off, as indicated by Figure 22.2(a). With the twostep mode the control action is discontinuous. A consequence of this is that oscillations of the controlled variable occur about the required condition. This is because of lags in the time that the control system and the process take to respond. For example, in the case of the temperature control for a domestic central heating system, when the room temperature drops below the required level, the time that elapses before the control system responds and switches the heater on might be very small in comparison with the time that elapses before the heater begins to have an effect on the room temperature. In the meantime, the temperature has fallen even more. The reverse situation occurs when the temperature has risen to the required temperature. Since time elapses before the control system reacts and switches the heater off, and yet more time while the heater cools and stops heating the room, the room temperature goes beyond the required value. The result is that the room temperature oscillates above and below the required temperature (Figure 22.2(b)). With the simple two-step system described above, there is the problem that when the room temperature is hovering about the set value the thermostat might be almost continually switching on or off, reacting to very slight changes in temperature. This can be avoided if, instead of just a single temperature value at which the controller switches the heater on or off, two values are used and the heater is switched on at a lower temperature than the one at which it is switched off (Figure 22.2(c)). The term dead band is used for the values between the on and off values. A large dead band results in large fluctuations of the temperature about the set temperature; a small dead band will result in an increased frequency of switching. The bimetallic element shown in Figure 2.46 has a permanent magnet for a switch contact; this has the effect of producing a dead band. Two-step control action tends to be used where changes are taking place very slowly, i.e. with a process with a large capacitance. Thus, in the case of heating a room, the effect on the room temperature of switching the heater on or off is only a slow change. The result of this is an oscillation with a long periodic time. Two-step control is thus not very precise, but it does involve simple devices and is thus fairly cheap. On/off control is not restricted to mechanical switches such as bimetallic strips or relays; rapid switching can be achieved with the use of thyristor circuits (see Section 9.3.2); such a circuit might be used for controlling the speed of a motor, and operational amplifiers. ylppus retaeH erutarepmeT Controller switch positions Heater supply Time On On On Off Off

550 CHAPTER 22 CLOSED-LOOP CONTROLLERS An example of a commercially available on/off controller is OMEGA’s TSW series adjustable temperature switch which consists of a sealed enclosure filled with a gas that expands as the temperature rises. A membrane within the switch body then mechanically causes closure of a relay. A range of such on/off switches is available for operation over different temperature ranges and having different contact ratings, e.g. TSW-35 for the range -5 to 35°C with a repeatability of {0.8°C and a contact rating of 5 A. 22.3 Proportional mode of control With the two-step method of control, the controller output is either an on or an off signal, regardless of the magnitude of the error. With the proportional mode, the size of the controller output is proportional to the size of the error: the bigger the error, the bigger the output from the controller. This means that the correction element of the control system will receive a signal which is proportional to the size of the correction required. For example, a proportional controller used for controlling temperature decreases the average power supplied to the heater as the temperature approaches the set point. This has the effect of slowing down the heater, so ideally the temperature will not overshoot the set point but will approach the set point and reach and maintain a stable temperature. With proportional control, for feedback of a correcting signal to occur there has to be an error, so a proportional control system always has a steady-state error. Increasing the gain results in the response speed of the system increasing and decreases the steady-state error, but if the proportional gain is set too high then overshoot can occur and if the controller were repeatedly to make changes that were too large, it would repeatedly overshoot the required value and the output would oscillate around the set point. As an illustration, if you were taking a shower you might turn it on and cold water emerges, so you switch up the temperature control. As you notice the water temperature increasing and anticipating further temperature increase you might switch the temperature control down before you reach the required water temperature. However, without such anticipation the water temperature will overshoot the required temperature. A proportional controller is not like a human and does not anticipate the changes that are coming. For a proportional controller, we must have controller output = K e P where e is the error and K the proportionality constant. Thus, taking Laplace P transforms, controller output (s) = K E(s) P and so K is the transfer function of the proportional controller P (Figure 22.3). Figure 22.3 Block diagram of Laplace domain proportional control. Controller E(s) output(s) K p M22 Mechatronics 50977.indd 550 29/08/2018 18:16

22.3 PROPORTIONAL MODE OF CONTROL 551 A commercial proportional controller that is used to control temperature, instead of controlling the temperature by decreasing the power supplied to a heater, is likely to exercise control by turning the heater output on and off for short time intervals rather than varying the correction heater temperature. This varies the ratio of on time to off time to control the temperature. At the set point the on time and off time are equal. The on and off times vary in proportion to the temperature difference from the set point. This proportioning action occurs within a proportional band around the set point temperature. Outside this band, the controller functions as an on/off unit, with the output either fully on when below the band or fully off when above the band. With a proportional control feedback system we have a gain element with transfer function K in series with the forward path element with transfer P function G (s) (Figure 22.4). The error is thus given by (see Section 20.5) P G (s)K R(s) P P E(s) = R(s) - X(s) = R(s) 1 + K G(s) P and so the error E(s) is 1 E(s) = R(s) 1 + K G(s) P If we have a unit step input, then R(s) = 1 s and so the steady-state error is 1 1 > E(s) = 1 + K G (s) s P P The steady-state error e will be the error after all transients have died away. ss We can obtain this by using the final-value theorem (see Appendix A). Increasing the gain of the controller reduces the magnitude of the steady-state error; however, this can affect system stability if the gain is increased too much. Thus, proportional-only controllers are limited to processes where the gain can be made large enough to reduce the steady-state error to acceptable limits without becoming unstable. However, integral control is effective as it produces correction even when the error is small because it takes account of what the error had previously been. It does, however, when compared with just proportional mode, increase output overshoot past the set point and so increases the settling time. Integral control will also give a correction when a controller is required to correct for a steady and continuous error from the required signal level by producing correction which increases, the longer the error persists. 22.7 DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS 559 22.7 Digital control systems So far in this chapter the control systems discussed and analysed have been analogue control systems to implement some control law, such as giving an output which is proportional to the size of the input. However, we also need to consider digital control systems, as when a microprocessor is used to implement a control law. Microprocessors require inputs which are digital signals and give outputs which are digital (Figure 21.16(a)). Thus, where the incoming signal from is analogue there has to be an analogue-to-digital converter (ADC) between the incoming signal and the microprocessor. The output from the microprocessor is digital and thus as most control systems use correction elements which operate on analogue signals there has to be a digital-toanalogue converter (DAC). Such control systems are basically of the form described in Figure 22.16(b) and (c). The ADC and the DAC may be incorporated with the microprocessor in a microcontroller. With the closed-loop control system the microprocessor compares the digital versions of the required set value with the digital version of feedback value from the measurement system which is monitoring the output. The microprocessor then implements the control mode for which it has been programmed before giving an output which, usually after conversion to analogue, can be used to operate the correction element. This section is about such open- and closed-loop systems. 560 CHAPTER 22 CLOSED-LOOP CONTROLLERS 22.7.1 Sampled data systems The microprocessor is operating what is often termed a sampled data system in that the ADC (see Section 4.2) supplying its input is a sampling system, being controlled by a clock giving regular time signals so that it takes samples of its analogue input every time it receives a time signal. A simple way of considering this is as a switch which is being switched on and off at regular time intervals to determine when values of an input are to be supplied to the microprocessor (Figure 22.17(a)). The samples which are taken are held for the time interval until the next sample is taken. The output is thus a series of sampled pulses representing the analogue input at regular time intervals. These samples are then converted into digital values to be operated on by the program in the microprocessor. Thus, if we have a continuous input to the sampler (Figure 22.17(b)) the output from it is as illustrated in Figure 22.17(c). Because the input is a constant value signal the output is a sequence of constant value pulses. Thus, we might have a constant output from the ADC of the digital signal 001. Whatever the form of the input, the sampled signal will be a sequence of impulses with each impulse in the sequence being multiplied by the value of the function at the time it is sampled. 562 CHAPTER 22 CLOSED-LOOP CONTROLLERS Input Output ADC giving DAC with its analogue Microprocessor analogue sampled signals zero-order hold signal signal Figure 22.21 From input to the ADC to the microprocessor and then to the DAC and its output. 22.7.2 Digital controllers Figure 22.16(c) shows the basis of a digital control system that can be used with a continuous process, a microprocessor being in control of the closedloop control system. The controller receives inputs from sensors, executes control programs and provides the output to the correction elements. The microprocessor can then initiate a control mode to process the error signal and give a digital output. The control mode used by the microprocessor is determined by the program of instructions used by the microprocessor for processing the digital signals, i.e. the software. The digital output, generally after processing by a DAC since correcting elements generally require analogue signals, can be used to initiate the correcting action. A digital controller basically operates the following cycle of events: 1 Samples the measured value. 2 Compares it with the set value and establishes the error. 3 Carries out calculations based on the error value and stored values of previous inputs and outputs to obtain the output signal. 4 Sends the output signal to the DAC. 5 Waits until the next sample time before repeating the cycle. Microprocessors as controllers have the advantage over analogue controllers that the form of the controlling action, e.g. proportional or three-mode, can be altered by purely a change in the computer software. No change in hardware or electrical wiring is required. Indeed, the control strategy can be altered by the computer program during the control action in response to the developing situation. There are also other advantages. With analogue control, separate controllers are required for each process being controlled. With a microprocessor, many separate processes can be controlled by sampling processes with a multiplexer (see Section 4.4). Digital control gives better accuracy than analogue control because the amplifiers and other components used with analogue systems change their characteristics with time and temperature and so show drift, while digital control, because it operates on signals in only the on/off mode, does not suffer from drift in the same way. 22.7.3 Implementing control modes In order to produce a digital controller which will give a particular mode of control it is necessary to produce a suitable program for the controller. The program has to indicate how the digital error signal at a particular instant is to be processed in order to arrive at the required output for the following correction element. The processing can involve the present input together with previous inputs and previous outputs. The program is thus asking the controller to carry out a difference equation (see Section 4.6). 564 CHAPTER 22 CLOSED-LOOP CONTROLLERS 22.8 Controller tuning The term tuning is used to describe the process of selecting the best controller settings. With a proportional controller, this means selecting the value of K ; with a PID controller the three constants K , K and K have to be P P I D selected. One method that can be used is: 1 Set all P, I and D gains to zero. 2 Increase the P gain until the response to a disturbance is steady oscillation. 3 Increase the D gain until the oscillations go away. 4 Repeat steps 2 and 3 until increasing the D gain does not stop the oscillations. 5 Set P and D to the last stable values. 6 Increase the I gain until the set point is arrived at with no oscillations. There are other methods of tuning; the following are two commonly used methods, both by Ziegler and Nichols. They assumed that when the controlled system is open loop a reasonable approximation to its behaviour is a first-order system with a built-in time delay. Based on this, they then derived parameters for optimum performance. This was taken to be settings which gave an under-damped transient response with a decay (subsidence) ratio of 1, i.e. the second overshoot is one-quarter of the first P 4 overshoot (see Section 19.5). This overshoot criterion gives a good compromise of small rise time, small settling time and a reasonable margin of stability. 0 Time For the process reaction method, the process control loop is opened, generally between the controller and the correction unit, so that no control action occurs. A test input signal is then applied to the correction unit and the M response of the controlled variable determined. The test signal should be as small as possible. Figure 22.23 shows the form of test signal and a typical response. The test signal is a step signal with a step size expressed as the percentage change P in the correction unit. The graph of the measured variable plotted against time is called the process reaction curve. The measured variable is expressed as the percentage of the full-scale range. 0 A tangent is drawn to give the maximum gradient of the graph. For Time T Figure 22.23 the maximum gradient R is M/T. The time between the Figure 22.23 Process reaction start of the test signal and the point at which this tangent intersects the curve. graph time axis is termed the lag L. Table 22.1 gives the criteria recommended by Ziegler and Nichols for control settings based on the values of P, R and L. Consider the following example. 566 CHAPTER 22 CLOSED-LOOP CONTROLLERS 22.9 Velocity control Consider the problem of controlling the movement of a load by means of a motor. Because the motor system is likely to be of second order, proportional control will lead to the system output taking time to reach the required displacement when there is, say, a step input to the system and may oscillate for a while about the required value. Time will thus be taken for the system to respond to an input signal. A higher speed of response, with fewer oscillations, can be obtained by using PD rather than just P control. There is, however, an alternative way of achieving the same effect and this is by the use of a second feedback loop which gives a measurement related to the rate at which the displacement is changing. This is termed velocity feedback. Figure 22.25 shows such a system; the velocity feedback might involve the use of a tachogenerator giving a signal proportional to the rotational speed of the motor shaft, and hence the rate at which the displacement is changing, and the displacement might be monitored using a rotary potentiometer or optical encoder. 22.10 ADAPTIVE CONTROL 567 22.10 Adaptive control There are many control situations where the parameters of the plant change with time or, perhaps, load. A robot manipulator can have large, rigid, heavy links and as such gives a manipulator for which the size of load will have little effect. However, a lightweight and flexible robot will have links that have a flexibility which markedly changes with load. For such a manipulator, the control parameters will depend on the load. If the transfer function of the plant changes then retuning of the system is desirable for the optimum values to be determined for proportional, derivative and integral constants. For the control systems so far considered, it has been assumed that the system once tuned retains its values of proportional, derivative and integral constants until the operator decides to retune. The alternative to this is an adaptive control system which ‘adapts’ to changes and changes its parameters to fit the circumstances prevailing. The adaptive control system is based on the use of a microprocessor as the controller. Such a device enables the control mode and the control parameters used to be adapted to fit the circumstances, modifying them as the circumstances change. An adaptive control system can be considered to have three stages of operation: 1 Starts to operate with controller conditions set on the basis of an assumed condition. 2 The desired performance is continuously compared with the actual system performance. 3 The control system mode and parameters are automatically and continuously adjusted in order to minimise the difference between the desired and actual system performance. For example, with a control system operating in the proportional mode, the proportional constant K may be automatically adjusted to fit the circumP stances, changing as they do. Adaptive control systems can take a number of forms. Three commonly used forms are: 1 Gain-scheduled control or, as it is sometimes referred to, preprogrammed adaptive control, has preset changes in the parameters of the controller made on the basis of some auxiliary measurement of some process variable. Figure 22.26 illustrates this method. The term gainscheduled control was used because the only parameter originally adjusted was the gain, i.e. the proportionality constant K . For example, for a conP trol system used to control the positioning of some load, the system parameters could be worked out for a number of different load values and a table of values loaded into the memory of the controller. A load cell might Figure 22.26 Gain-scheduled Auxiliary control. measurement Adjustment Output Controller Correction Process Measurement M22 Mechatronics 50977.indd 567 29/08/2018 18:16

568 CHAPTER 22 CLOSED-LOOP CONTROLLERS then be used to measure the actual load and give a signal to the controller indicating a mass value which is then used by the controller to select the appropriate parameters. A disadvantage of this system is that the control parameters have to be determined for many operating conditions so that the controller can select the one to fit the prevailing conditions. An advantage, however, is that the changes in the parameters can be made quickly when the conditions change. 2 Self-tuning control is where the system continuously tunes its own parameters based on monitoring the variable that the system is controlling and the output from the controller. Figure 22.27 illustrates this method. Self-tuning is often found in commercial PID controllers, generally then being referred to as auto tuning. When the operator presses a button, the controller injects a small disturbance into the system and measures the response. This response is compared with the desired response and the control parameters adjusted, by a modified Ziegler–Nichols rule, to bring the actual response closer to the desired response. 3 Model-reference adaptive system involves an accurate model of the system being developed. The set value is then used as an input to both the actual and the model systems and the difference between the actual output and the output from the model compared. Figure 22.28 illustrates this method. The difference in these signals is then used to adjust the parameters of the controller to minimise the difference. Figure 22.27 Self-tuning. Artificial intelligence Chapter twenty three Objectives The objectives of this chapter are that, after studying it, the reader should be able to: • Explain what is meant by an intelligent machine and the capabilities of such machines. • Explain the meaning of artificial neural networks (ANNs) and their relevance to pattern recognition. • Explain the term fuzzy logic and its applications in modern machines. 23.1 What is meant by artificial What constitutes an intelligent machine? A dictionary definition of intelligent intelligence? might be ‘endowed with the ability to reason’. The more intelligent we think a person is, the more we consider they are able to learn, generalise from this acquired knowledge, be capable of reasoning and able to make predictions by considering what is possible, learning from any mistakes. We can apply the same criteria to a machine: an intelligent machine is one endowed with the ability to reason. It uses sensors to monitor its environment and as a consequence adjusts its actions to accomplish tasks in the face of uncertainty and variability in its environment. With the human brain we have a biological network of neurons that learn, i.e. progressively improve their performance, by repeatedly considering images and using the results of each consideration to build up a knowledge base. For example, repeatedly looking at versions of the handwritten letter A enables the brain to learn what the characteristics of the letter are so that future versions can be recognised as being the letter A. This chapter includes a brief look at basic concepts associated with intelligent machines using artificial neural networks (ANNs) that can learn to operate in the same way and how such programs are beginning to find a use in our everyday lives. Traditionally, computers are used to making decisions on the basis of binary logic, i.e. YES there is a signal or NO there is no signal. When humans make decisions about some proposition it is likely they will include a range of possibilities between YES and NO and by getting computers to make decisions on such a range of possibilities has led to the development of fuzzy logic. A brief introduction to the principles of fuzzy logic is presented in this chapter. Fuzzy logic is now widely used in in the controls of domestic appliances such as washing machines. To analytically obtain a control law for a plant you need a mathematical model of that plant. A mathematical model for a plant is not, however, a necessary prerequisite when neural and fuzzy logic approaches are used to exercise control. M23 Mechatronics 50977.indd 571 29/08/2018 18:18

572 CHAPTER 23 ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE 23.2 Perception and cognition Perception is the collecting of information using sensors and the organising of the gathered information so that decisions can be made. For example, a control system used with a production line might have a number of sensors to observe components on a conveyor belt and detect features of those components. The signals received from the sensors enable a computed representation of the components to be made so that features can be identified. This will contain information about critical elements of the components which can then be compared with representations of the components so that decisions can be made by the control system as to whether the component is correctly assembled or perhaps which component it is. Then action can be taken by the control system perhaps to reject faulty components or divert particular components to particular boxes. Thus, with a mechatronics system, perception involves sensors gathering appropriate information about a system and its environment, decoding it and processing it to give useful information which can be used elsewhere in the system to make decisions. 23.2.1 Cognition Once a machine has collected and organised information, it has to make decisions about what to do as a consequence of the information gathered. This is termed cognition. Vital to this perception and cognition is pattern recognition. What are the patterns in the data gathered? Humans are very good at pattern recognition. Think of a security guard observing television monitors. The guard is able to look at the monitors and recognise unusual patterns, e.g. a person where there should be no person, an object having been moved, etc. This is the facility required of intelligent machines. An autopilot system on an aircraft monitors a lot of information and, on the basis of the patterns perceived in that data, makes decisions as to how to adjust the aircraft controls. Machine pattern recognition can be achieved by the machine having a set of patterns in its memory and gathered patterns are then compared with these and a match sought. The patterns in its memory may arise from models or a process of training in which data is gathered for a range of objects or situations and these given identification codes. For example, for recognising coins, information may be gathered about diameter and colour. Thus, a particular coin might be classified as circular and having a diameter of 2.25 cm and a colour which is a particular degree of redness (a bronze coin). However, an intelligent machine will need to take account of worn and dirty coins and still be able to recognise the coin. To recognise a handwritten number or signature the intelligent machine breaks up the number or signature into individual digits and letters. It then needs to identify each digit/letter symbol. It can do this by breaking the symbol into small segments and then taking all the results and, as a result of being trained by looking at many variations, finding the digit or number

It can do this by breaking the symbol into small segments and then taking all the results and, as a result of being trained by looking at many variations, finding the digit or number that best fits the results. Out of the processing of inputs by the network of neurons emerges the human mind that is capable of analysing and solving complex situations. Artificial neural networks (ANNs) have been developed which can begin to analyse input data and solve problems in a similar manner. ANNs are made up of ‘neurons’ connected by links (Figure 23.2). The ‘neurons’ can input data and Processors, i.e. the Neuron output if Network artificial neurons the sum of the weighted inputs connections Weightings exceeds the firing w1 threshold w2 Outputs Inputs w3 Input layer Weighted inputs from Output layer neurons to a hidden layer or output neuron Hidden layer Figure 23.2 Neural network. 574 CHAPTER 23 ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE Figure 23.3 Inputs and output Weightings from a hidden layer or output neuron. Inputs from x 1 2 Output if the sum of the neurons to a 3 weighted inputs exceeds the hidden layer x 2 firing threshold, e.g. 8. Thus, or output 4 output when 2x + 3x + 4x > 8 neuron x 1 2 3 3 perform simple operations on the data with the results being passed via the links onto other ‘neurons’ to carry out further operations. A neural network is made up of input neurons, hidden neurons and output neurons. Input neurons are designed to receive information from the outside world that the network will attempt to learn about, recognise or otherwise process. Output neurons are on the opposite side of the network and signal how it has responded to the information it has received. In between the input neurons and output neurons are one or more layers of hidden neurons, these being connected to neurons in the layers on either side. Incoming information is thus in the form of signals that are passed between neurons through connection links. Each connection link has a weight that multiplies the signal transmitted. The higher the weight, the more influence one neuron has on another. A neuron will give an output if the sum of its weighted inputs exceeds its firing threshold. Figure 23.3 illustrates this. The issue is then how do we determine the weighting factors to use? Supervised learning can be used with the information being supplied to the neurons in the form of a training set consisting of known input– output pairs. The weight of the connection between any two neurons is then gradually adjusted as the weights are changed so that the actual output of the system corresponds to the desired output. The network thus learns. When a neural network is learning or operating normally after being trained, patterns of information are fed into the network via the input neurons. These trigger the layers of hidden neurons with their inputs multiplied by the weights of the connections they travel along. Every neuron adds up all the inputs it receives in this way and if the sum is more than a certain threshold value, the unit ‘fires’ and triggers the neurons to which it is connected. This continues through the neurons until the output is reached. While more than one hidden layer might be beneficial with a neural network for some applications, often one hidden layer might be sufficient. There are no general rules about how many neurons should be in the hidden layer, but one rule that was developed by J. Yang, H. Rivard and R. Zmeureanu for building energy prediction using ANNs (Energy and Buildings, 2005, 37, 1250–1259) is number of hidden neurons = 2 * N + 1 i where N is the number of input neurons. i Neural networks need to be trained from examples before they can be put to use. Neural networks learn by

energy prediction using ANNs (Energy and Buildings, 2005, 37, 1250–1259) is number of hidden neurons = 2 * N + 1 i where N is the number of input neurons. i Neural networks need to be trained from examples before they can be put to use. Neural networks learn by a feedback process which involves comparing the output a network produces with the output it was meant to produce, and using the difference between them to modify the weights of the connections between the units in the network They ‘learn’ as more and more data is analysed by them and their outcomes compared with the real answers, so establishing when a pathway produces a correct answer and when it does not. The pathway can then be remembered for use when the same inputs occur again. Once the network has been trained with enough learning examples, it reaches M23 Mechatronics 50977.indd 574 29/08/2018 18:18

23.3 FUZZY LOGIC 575 a point where it can be presented with an entirely new set of inputs it has never seen before and its response observed. Handwriting recognition on a touchscreen is one of many applications perfectly suited to a neural network. Each character (letter, number or symbol) that is written is recognised on the basis of key features it contains, such as vertical lines, horizontal lines, angled lines, curves, etc., and the order in which they are drawn on the screen. Neural networks also find a use in photographic image recognition. Thus, the first layer of ‘neurons’ might analyse pixel brightness before passing the operation to a second layer which identifies edges and lines formed by strips of similar pixels, then passing onto a layer which identifies shapes, and so on. The outcome of such a sequence of operations can be recognition of an image having a particular set of characteristics. Google’s photos app for image recognition currently uses 30 different layers to make sense of images. It automatically analyses photos, identifying various visual features and subjects so that users can search for anything in photos, it recognising faces and grouping similar ones together, geographic landmarks and subjects such as birthdays, buildings, animals, etc. Microsoft’s Cortana uses a similar form of processing to produce answers to queries or situations. A neural network also finds an application in a control system as a controller. In a conventional on/off control system (see Section 22.2) the on and off conditions are fixed and do not change if conditions change, likewise for the controller in a PID control system (see Section 22.6). If, however, a neural network is used as the controller, it can be trained to recognise when conditions change and adjust its control action accordingly. For example, a heater control system for a room might have a neural controller with four input neurons: one for the room temperature, one for the change in room temperature during the last 10 minutes, one for outdoor temperature and one for the change in outdoor temperature during the last 10 minutes. After processing by a single layer of nine hidden layer neurons, there is just one output neuron which supplies a signal to change the room temperature in an on/off control. 23.3 Fuzzy logic Computers are used to making decisions on the basis of YES there is a signal or NO there is no signal. When humans make decisions about some proposition it is likely they will include a range of possibilities between YES and NO and so we can have: CERTAINLY YES POSSIBLY YES MAYBE POSSIBLY NO CERTAINLY NO so there is a degree of fuzziness about data fitting a proposition. Lotfi Zadeh proposed in 1965 a form of reasoning based on fuzzy set theory which has become known as fuzzy logic. Fuzzy logic copies this aspect of human decision making with its range of ‘fuzzy’ possibilities by considering all available information and making the best possible decision given the input. 576 CHAPTER 23 ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE Figure 23.4 (a) Classical set theory, (b) fuzzy set theory. real-world knowledge in the face of uncertainty arising due to generality, vagueness, ambiguity, chance or incomplete knowledge. In classical set theory, an element either belongs to or does not belong to a set, but in a fuzzy set a degree of membership (between 0 and 1) is allowed. Thus, propositions need not be classified as true or false, but their truth or falsehood can be weighted so that it can be classified between the two on a 0 to 1 scale. As an illustration, consider the heights of people. In classical set theory a set, by definition, is a collection of things that belong to some definition. Consider as an example a set of tall people. We might say that people taller than or equal to 1.8 m are tall. This set can be represented by Figure 23.4(a) showing how the degree of membership of the tall set, or membership function as it is usually called, varies with height. A person with a height of 1.8 m or more has a membership function of 1.0 and is definitely classed as tall and in the tall set. A person with a height less than 1.8 m has a membership function of 0 and is definitely not tall and not in the tall set. Thus, even if the person was only 1 cm less than 1.8 m they would not be in the tall set. With classical sets we have defined two sets, one for tall people and one for short people, and the sets do not overlap. A person is either in one set or the other. A person is thus either in or not in the tall set. With fuzzy sets we have overlapping sets so the membership function is of the form shown in Figure 23.4(b). The membership function defines the fuzzy set for the possible values underneath it on the horizontal axis. The vertical axis, on a scale of 0 to 1, provides the membership value of the height in the tall fuzzy set. So for a person of height 1.5 m there is a membership of 0.25 in the tall set and 0.75 in the short set, so having a low degree of membership of the fuzzy set of tall people, and thus is not very tall. A person with a membership function of 0.95 in the tall set and 0.05 in the short set thus has a high degree of membership in the fuzzy set of tall people, and so is pretty definitely tall.
